title: Portfolio Management and Investment Advice

narrative.executive_view: Independent wealth-advice RIAs offer meaningful AI productivity potential in planning preparation, client-service operations and investment workflow, but not a clean cost-free roll-up. Fees are tied to AUM rather than hours, helping retention of savings; at the same time buyers compete fiercely for transferable client books and client-consent rules make retention central. The code is heterogeneous, so this packet scores wealth-advice RIAs rather than institutional asset managers. [S1][S2][S5]
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: AI can draft meeting notes, plans, emails, research summaries, service workflows and CRM updates. It is less able to replace relationship-based advice, behavioral coaching, fiduciary accountability and rainmaking. Schwab reports 68% of firms using AI in some way in its 2025 benchmarking study, while its later RIA-focused survey says use is concentrated in administrative tasks and client communication. [S1][S6]
narrative.value_capture: AUM fees mean a reduced back-office cost does not automatically reprice the client contract, so a firm can initially retain part of savings. The counterweight is fee compression, robo/AI alternatives and portable founder relationships. pi_dist therefore reflects partial retention, and pi_moat reflects client trust and institutionalized service—not the regulatory human-accountability floor, which belongs to terminal demand.
narrative.adoption_timing: The 68% broad AI-use result is above the mechanical 50% threshold, but it includes experimentation and does not establish 50% effective task adoption. The dedicated 2026 survey says most are still experimenting. I select 45% effective adoption and a positive two-year path to 50%, explicitly separating use from integrated savings. [S1][S6]
narrative.consolidation_economics: Deal volume and the buyer universe are strong, which helps liquidity but weakens entry arbitrage. ECHELON reports record 2025 volume and more unique buyers. AGS's transaction report directly labels its 2024 11.0x market median as adjusted EBITDA, which makes it a denominator-consistent exit benchmark rather than a headline valuation statistic. It remains a broad transaction median, not a promise that a founder-dependent $1–10M-EBITDA purchase exits at that level; the 8x entry is a conservative estimate after matching owner compensation and retention economics. [S3][S4]
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is PRESSURED, not melting. Human advisers retain value in complex planning, trust and accountability, and advisory contracts cannot generally be assigned without client consent. Yet AI makes a conversational substitute more credible and puts pressure on the visible, routine parts of a 1%-of-AUM proposition. [S5]
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The dataset has three weak priors: ancestor-level labor share, 3-digit brokerage-contaminated role mix, and establishment count rather than firms. The required target-count fallback is a transparent estimate based on the Form ADV adviser census and small-firm distribution, not a NAICS census. M is fragile even after denominator repair: the observed 11.0x is an adjusted-EBITDA market median, while a small founder-dependent target may require a discount for owner replacement, client retention and cohort mix.

sources.title: 2025 RIA Benchmarking Study | Charles Schwab
sources.what_it_supports: Schwab's 1,288-firm 2025 study reports 68% of firms use AI in some way; the data are self-reported.

sources.title: Investment Adviser Association Industry Statistics
sources.what_it_supports: 2026 IAA/COMPLY Form ADV snapshot reports 16,544 advisers in 2025, 92.8% with 100 or fewer employees, 67.4% below $1B AUM, and individual-client firms averaging 8 employees and $424M AUM.

sources.title: ECHELON 2025 RIA M&A Deal Report
sources.what_it_supports: ECHELON reports 2025 wealth-management deal volume above 400 and a 17.6% year-over-year increase in unique buyers; it also describes elevated smaller transaction demand.

sources.title: The RIA Deal Room 2025 | Advisor Growth Strategies
sources.what_it_supports: AGS reports that its 2024 RIA transaction data had a median adjusted EBITDA multiple of 11.0x, up 11% from 2023; it also explains that valuation rises with scale and seller quality, and that the largest platforms can command 20x adjusted EBITDA or more.

sources.title: SEC Investment Management Staff Issues of Interest — Advisory Contracts
sources.what_it_supports: SEC describes Advisers Act section 205(a)(2) contract provisions requiring client consent to assignment for registered advisers.

sources.title: 2026 RIA & AI Research Study: Advisor AI in Action | Charles Schwab
sources.what_it_supports: Schwab's October 2025 survey of 533 advisors reports 63% use AI, most are experimenting, and impact is most felt in administrative tasks and client communications.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 5239 ($166,457,424,125) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 5239 ($454,212,096,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 5239. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Securities, Commodities, and Financial Services Sales Agents
Executive Secretaries and Executive Administrative Assistants

n_total.source: QCEW 2024 private annual avg establishments (SUSB out of scope for this code; establishments are a proxy for firms — overcounts multi-unit firms)

n_band.rationale: Start with 16,544 SEC advisers; 67.4% are below $1B AUM (11,151). Individual-client firms average $424M AUM; at a 0.8% realized advisory fee they generate about $3.4M revenue, and applying the supplied 11.65% margin yields about $0.39M EBITDA. The $1–10M EBITDA band therefore requires the upper part of the sub-$1B population and larger firms; 4,000 is a judgmental count allowing for higher AUM, fee and margin firms while excluding institutional managers and state-registered micro-firms.
caveats: Form ADV adviser census is not NAICS 523940 and excludes state-registered advisers.
The 0.8% fee and distribution within AUM bands are judgmental bridges; no published AUM-band table was reopened.
The supplied 11.65% margin is broad asset-management, likely below standardized wealth-RIA margins; n_band is intentionally low confidence.

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: AI targets documented administrative and client-communication workflows while client relationships, fiduciary judgment and rainmaking remain.
caveats: Supplied role mix is 3-digit 523 and includes brokerage/asset-management contamination.
AI use is not equivalent to headcount removal.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Planning drafts, meeting prep and reporting automate; relationship and advice accountability persist.

breakdown_by_role.role: Securities, Commodities, and Financial Services Sales Agents
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Prospecting support and paperwork automate; relationship sales persist.
Research, screening and reporting are highly automatable; investment accountability remains.
Billing, reporting and operations automate; supervisory duties persist.
Management analysis automates partly; leadership and compliance accountability remain.
Routine office workflow is highly exposed.
Scheduling and routine service requests automate, escalation remains human.
Reconciliation and reporting automate but review/accountability remain.
Coding assistance does not remove system ownership and oversight.

breakdown_by_role.role: Executive Secretaries and Executive Administrative Assistants
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Calendar, notes and correspondence workflow is highly exposed.
Residual blends portfolio-manager and service/ops tails; use a midrange judgment.

pi_dist.rationale: AUM-based fees decouple price from staffing cost, retaining some gains; fee compression and competition prevent full retention.
caveats: Fee schedules and client mix are target-specific.

pi_moat.rationale: Trust, planning complexity and contracts support retention, but AI-native advice and portable advisor relationships constrain it.
caveats: Client-consent rules create retention risk, not automatic moat.

t50_years.rationale: Use is widespread but most firms are experimenting; two years estimates the move to 50% effective, integrated workflow adoption.
caveats: Measured use is above 50%, but effective adoption is a stricter construct.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: 68% report some use and 63% of advisors report use, but both sources say use is early/experimental; 45% estimates effective task adoption.
caveats: Not a direct census measure; prevents incorrectly setting t50 to zero.

historical_analogs.value: Robo and digital-advice unbundling: routine allocation/reporting becomes cheaper while complex planning and trusted relationship service retain a paid segment.
historical_analogs.rationale: Sources show AI first affects administrative and communication work, consistent with unbundling rather than immediate replacement.
caveats: Not a causal estimate of future fee compression.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: Small-adviser population creates succession opportunities, but IAA does not publish owner-age share in the reopened page.
caveats: No direct owner-age statistic; do not treat adviser demographics as owner demographics.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: ECHELON reports a growing buyer universe and smaller firms moving toward partnerships, documenting succession/partnership demand rather than a numerical shortage.
caveats: This is market commentary, not a code-specific succession survey.

active_consolidators.rationale: Record deal volume, 17.6% more unique buyers and AGS's documented transaction-market competition support a high active-buyer count.
caveats: Count is an estimate of active platforms/strategics, not reported by either source.

buy_mult.rationale: Use an 8x adjusted-EBITDA entry estimate below AGS's 11.0x adjusted-EBITDA market median because the target is a founder-dependent $1–10M EBITDA business and requires owner-compensation and retention normalization.
caveats: AGS's transaction median is not a size-matched small-firm entry comp; quality small sellers may trade nearer the market median.
Owner compensation and contingent retention economics must be normalized before using this estimate in a live deal.

exit_mult.rationale: AGS's primary 2025 RIA Deal Room explicitly reports an 11.0x median adjusted EBITDA multiple for its 2024 RIA transaction data. Use that observed, denominator-matched market median as the exit benchmark, not a revenue, EBOC or undefined headline multiple.
caveats: The observed median spans RIA transactions and is not a size- or quality-matched forecast for a particular consolidated exit.
The report states larger and higher-quality firms receive higher valuations; a founder-dependent small target may require a discount after owner-compensation and client-retention normalization.

pricing_structure.value: Recurring AUM fees, often paired with planning/service, price advice as a percentage of managed assets rather than by labor hour; visible AI work increases fee-compression and unbundling risk.
pricing_structure.rationale: Schwab benchmarking covers products, pricing and technology, while AI evidence identifies the workflows clients may perceive as commoditized.
caveats: No exact fee-rate selection is used in scoring.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: High office/task exposure can be adopted faster than physical trades, but the positive two-year t50 recognizes regulated data, integration and trust constraints.

terminal_value.justification: Human advice retains trust and accountability value, but AI threatens the visible planning/communication layer and fee justification; contract assignment also makes revenue retention a real risk. [S5][S6]

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No: NAICS blends wealth advice and institutional asset management; role mix and labor share are broad proxies, and n_band is an explicit low-confidence fallback.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: AUM pricing initially protects savings, but fee compression, AI-native substitutes and client portability can return savings to clients or acquisition competition.

dataset_mismatch.description: Labor share is ancestor 5239 LOW quality; role mix is 3-digit 523 with brokerage contamination; n_total is establishments rather than firms; n_band is a researched estimate under the prompt exception.
sensitivity: Using wealth-RIA margins above the supplied broad 11.65% margin would raise band eligibility.
Using only SEC advisers excludes state-registered micro-firms, most likely below the band.

market_boundary.selection_basis: IAA reports a large small-adviser population and individual-client firms averaging 8 employees/$424M AUM; score the dominant firm-count segment rather than asset-manager economics. [S2]

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Effective adoption versus experimentation changes the time penalty.
Fallback drives target-density/buyability and is not a NAICS census.
A move to the observed market median materially erodes multiple arbitrage.

reviewer_note: Attempt-2 remediation: replace the prior undefined 11x headline valuation with AGS's reopened primary report, which explicitly states an 11.0x median adjusted EBITDA multiple. The 8x entry estimate is now stated on the same adjusted-EBITDA basis and retains owner-compensation, retention and size-mismatch caveats. No EBOC or revenue multiple is treated as EBITDA. All listed score-driving URLs were reopened on the run date.

A.arithmetic: Python v3.1.2: A = 10 when t50=0 else min(10,10/t50); t50=2, A=5
